hero_image.caption: Ida B. Wells-Barnett.
hero_image.repository: National Portrait Gallery (United States); via Google Arts & Culture
hero_image.license: Public domain (published before January 1, 1931; faithful reproduction of a 2D public-domain work)

inline_images.alt: Head-and-shoulders portrait of Ida B. Wells, 1891
inline_images.caption: Ida B. Wells in 1891, around the time she launched the anti-lynching documentation campaign that produced The Red Record (1895).
inline_images.photographer: Unknown (published in I. Garland Penn, The Afro-American Press and Its Editors, 1891)
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (LCCN 93505758)
inline_images.license: Public domain (published before 1929; no known restrictions on publication)

related_chapters.subject: Ellie Dahmer
related_chapters.why: Wells documents racial terror against families who claimed their rights, and Dahmer recounts the night watches before the Klan firebombing that killed her husband, the Forrest County NAACP president.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.part_title: Legacy and the Work That Remains
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Great-Uncle Lynched, and Restorative Justice
related_chapters.why: Wells compiled the record of lynching to force a reckoning, and Magee speaks of a great-uncle lynched and of pursuing justice for it.

related_chapters.subject: Cecil J. Williams
related_chapters.why: Wells's method was documentary witness against racial violence, and Williams describes photographing that violence so a future record could not be denied.

related_chapters.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
related_chapters.why: Wells closed The Red Record arguing a Winchester in the home was a Black family's rightful protection, and Avery speaks plainly of the shotgun as self-defense.

related_chapters.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
related_chapters.why: Wells held that an unprotected people must defend itself, and the Andersons describe the Deacons for Defense organizing armed protection in Bogalusa.

related_chapters.subject: Euvester Simpson
related_chapters.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
related_chapters.why: Wells records the suffering inflicted in custody on Black people charged with nothing, and Simpson recounts the night of the Winona jail beating she endured with Fannie Lou Hamer.

top_voice.subject: Ellie Dahmer
top_voice.reason: Dahmer's testimony is the living counterpart to Wells's ledger of racial murder: she survived the Klan firebombing that killed her husband for offering to pay others' poll taxes.

closing_image.alt: Vernon Dahmer Sr. funeral, Shady Grove Baptist Church, Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 15, 1966
closing_image.caption: Vernon Dahmer Sr. funeral, Shady Grove Baptist Church, Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 15, 1966. Ellie Dahmer is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.
closing_image.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History
closing_image.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions The Red Record: Tabulated Statistics and Alleged Causes of Lynching in the United States raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Mildred Bond Roxborough, Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner), and Charles McLaurin.

nearest_voices.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
